questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: If $A B C D$ is a rectangle and $P$ is a point inside it such that $A P=33, B P=16, D P=63$. Find $C P$.
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { In } \triangle P A D, x^2+y^2=33^2 ...(i) \\ & \text { In } \triangle P B D,(a-x)^2+y^2=16^2 ...(ii)\\ & \text { In } \triangle P D F, x^2+(b-y)^2=63^2 ....(iii)\\ & \begin{array}{r} \text { (ii) }+(\text { iii })-(\text { i }) \Rightarrow(a-x)^2+(b-y)^2 \\ \quad=63^2+16^2-33^2 \end{array} \\ & C P^2=56^2 \quad\left[\because \text { In } \triangle P C E, C P^2=(a-x)^2+(b-y)^2\right] \\ & C P=56 \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: A bug travels in the coordinate plane moving only along the lines that are parallel to the $x$ axis or $y$ axis. Let $A=(-3,2)$ and $B(3,-2)$. Consider all possible paths of the bug from $A$ to $B$ of length at most 14 . How many points with integer coordinates lie on at least one of these paths?
questions.solution:
Least number of steps required to move from $A$ to $B$ is 10 . Let we denote any step towards positive $x$ axis by $R$ and negative $y$-axis by $D$.
So all steps are the permutations of $R R R R R R D D D$. In this way, all points of the rectangular grid are covered. If we introduce one step in negative $x$ axis or positive $y$ axis, movement will be completed in 12 steps. Similarly, if we introduce two steps in negative $x$-axis or in positive $y$-axis or one in each direction the movement will be completed in 14 steps (maximum allowed).
Maximum number of points with integer coordinates $=9 \times 7+7 \times 2+5 \times 2=87$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Let $A B C$ be a triangle in the $x y$ plane, where $B$ is at the origin $(0,0)$. Let $B C$ be produced to $D$ such that $B C: C D=1: 1, C A$ be produced to $E$ such that $C A: A E=1: 2$ and $A B$ be produced to $F$ such that $A B: B F=1: 3$. Let $G(32,24)$ be the centroid of the triangle $A B C$ and $K$ be the centroid of the triangle $D E F$. Find the length $G K$.
questions.solution: $\because B C: C D=1: 1 \quad$ hence $D=\left(2 x_1, 2 y_1\right)$ $C A: A E=1: 2 \quad$ hence $E=\left(3 x_2-2 x_1, 3 y_2-2 y_1\right)$ $A B: B F=1: 3 \quad$ hence $F=\left(-3 x_2,-3 y_2\right)$ $\therefore \quad$ Centroid of $\triangle D E F=K=(0,0)$ Centroid of $\triangle A B C=G=(32,24)$ $\therefore \quad G K=\sqrt{32^2+24^2}=40$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Three parallel lines $L_1, L_2, L_3$ are drawn in the plane such that the perpendicular distance between $L_1$ and $L_2$ is 3 and the perpendicular distance between $L_2$ and $L_3$ is also 3 . A square $A B C D$ is constructed such that $A$ lies on $L_1, B$ lies on $L_3$ and $C$ lies on $L_2$.Find the area of the square

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Let $A B C D$ be a rectangle in which $A B+B C+ C D=20$ and $A E=9$ where $E$ is the mid-point of the side $B C$. Find the area of the rectangle.
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & 2 x+y=20 \\ & y=20-2 x ...(i)\\ & \text { In } \triangle A B E \end{aligned}$ $x^2+\left(\frac{y}{2}\right)^2=9^2$...(ii) Using (i) in (ii) $\begin{aligned} & x^2+\left(\frac{20-2 x}{2}\right)^2=81 \\ & x^2+(10-x)^2=81 \\ & 2 x^2-20 x+19=0 \\ & x=\frac{20 \pm \sqrt{400-4 \times 2 \times 19}}{2 \times 2} \\ & x=\frac{10 \pm \sqrt{62}}{2} \end{aligned}$ From (i) $\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad y & =20-2\left(\frac{10 \pm \sqrt{62}}{2}\right) \\ y & =10 \mp \sqrt{62} \end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} \text { Area of rectangle }=x y & =\frac{1}{2}(10 \pm \sqrt{62})(10 \mp \sqrt{62}) \\ & =\frac{100-62}{2} \\ & =19\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Let $A B C D$ be a unit square. Suppose $M$ and $N$ are points on $B C$ and $C D$ respectively such that the perimeter of triangle $M C N$ is 2 . Let $O$ be the circumcentre of triangle $M A N$ and $P$ be the circumcentre of triangle $M O N$. If $\left(\frac{O P}{O A}\right)^2=\frac{m}{n}$ for some relatively prime positive integers $m$ and $n$, find the value of $m+n$.
questions.solution:
$\because \quad O A=$ circumradius of $\triangle A M N=\frac{M N}{2 \sin \alpha}$ $O P=$ circumradius of $\triangle O M N=\frac{M N}{2 \sin 2 \alpha}$ So $\left(\frac{O P}{O A}\right)^2=\left(\frac{1}{2 \cos \alpha}\right)^2$ Perimeter of $\triangle M C N=2=(1-x)+(1-y)+M N$ $\Rightarrow \quad M N=x+y$ Now rotate $\triangle A B M$ about $A$ so that $A B$ overlaps with $A D$ (by $90^{\circ}$ ) Clearly $\triangle A M N=\triangle A M^{\prime} N$ So $2 \alpha=90^{\circ}$ $\therefore \quad \alpha=45^{\circ}$
Hence $\left(\frac{O P}{O A}\right)^2=\frac{1}{2}=\frac{m}{n}$ $\therefore \quad m+n=3$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: In the coordinate plane, a point is called a lattice point if both of its coordinates are integers. Let $A$ be the point $(12,84)$. Find the number of right angled triangles $A B C$ in the coordinate plane where $B$ and $C$ are lattice points, having a right angle at the vertex $A$ and whose incenter is at the origin $(0,0)$.
questions.solution:
We know that if inradius is integer then sides of triangle is also integer.
Here $O A=60 \sqrt{2}$. Inradius $=60$ Hence $A B, B C, C A$ are integer. Let $A B=m^2-n^2$ $B C=m^2+n^2$ and $A C=2 m^2+n^2$ Here, $r=\frac{\Delta}{s}=\frac{m n \cdot\left(m^2-n^2\right)}{3(m+n)}=n(m-n)=60$ $\therefore n(m-n)=2^2 .3 .5 .$ $\therefore$ Possible values of $n$ are 12 . Then 12 such triangles are possible but when $B$ and $C$ interchange their position 12 more triangles are possible. Then total number of distinct triangles $=24$.

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: Consider a square $A B C D$ of side length 16 . Let $E, F$ be points on $C D$ such that $C E=E F=F D$. Let the line BF and $A E$ meet in $M$. The area of $\triangle M A B$ is:
questions.solution:
Eq of line $A E: 2 y-3 x+16=0$
Eq of line $B F$ : $2 y+3 x-32=0$ $\Rightarrow M \equiv(8,4)$ Area of triangle $A M B$ $\begin{aligned}\left|\frac{1}{2}\right| \begin{array}{ccc}0 & 16 & 1 \\ 16 & 16 & 1 \\ 8 & 4 & 1\end{array}|\mid & =\frac{1}{2}(-16(8)+1(64-8 \times 16)) \\ & =\left|\frac{1}{2}(-16 \times 16+64)\right| \\ & =\left|\frac{16}{2}(4-16)\right|=8 \times 12 \\ & =96 \text { sq.units }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: In a rectangle $A B C D, E$ is the midpoint of $A B: F$ is point on $A C$ such that $B F$ is perpendicular to $A C$ and $F E$ perpendicular to $B D$. Suppose $B C=8 \sqrt{3}$. Find $A B$.
questions.solution:
$\sin \theta=\frac{8 \sqrt{3}}{2 x}=\frac{4 \sqrt{3}}{x}$
$\begin{aligned} & F A=2 x \cos \theta \\ & F B=2 x \sin \theta \\ & M B=F B x \sin 2 \theta=2 x \sin \theta \sin 2 \theta \\ & M E^2+M B^2=B E^2=x^2 \\ & x^2 \sin ^2 \theta+4 x^2 \sin ^2 \theta \sin ^2 2 \theta=x^2 \\ & \sin ^2 \theta+4 \sin ^2 \theta \sin ^2 2 \theta=1 \\ & 4 \sin ^2 \theta \sin ^2 2 \theta=\cos ^2 \theta \\ & 16 \sin ^4 \theta=1 \\ & \sin ^2 \theta=\frac{1}{4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sin \theta=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \frac{4 \sqrt{3}}{x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad x=12 \Rightarrow 2 x=A B=24 \text { Ans. } \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: An ant leaves the anthill for its morning exercise. It walks 4 feet east and then makes a $160^{\circ}$ turn to the right and walks 4 more feet. It then makes another $160^{\circ}$ turn to the right and walks 4 more feet. If the ant continues this pattern until it reaches the anthill again, what is the distance in feet it would have walked?
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } A_0(0,0) \\ & A_1(4 \cos 0,4 \sin 0) \\ & A_2(4 \cos 0+4 \cos 160,4 \sin 0+4 \sin 160) \\ & A_n=(0,0) \\ & \Rightarrow 4(\cos 0+\cos 160+\ldots . .+\cos 160(n-1))=0 \text { and } 4(\sin 0+\sin 160+\ldots . .+\sin 160(n-1))=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \sin (80 n)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathbf{n}=\mathbf{9} \\ & \Rightarrow \text { distance } \text { covered }=4 \times 9=36 \text { feet }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: A point P in the interior of a regular hexagon is at distance $8,8,16$ units from three consecutive vertices of the hexagon, respectively. If $r$ is radius of the circumscribed circle of the hexagon, what is the integer closest to $r$ ?
questions.solution: Note that $\mathrm{CF}=2 \mathrm{AB}, \mathrm{PA}=2 \mathrm{PC}$ \& $\mathrm{PB}=2 \mathrm{PF}$ Hence $\triangle \mathrm{PAB}$ is similar to $\triangle \mathrm{PFC}$, hence $\mathrm{A}_1 \mathrm{P}_1 \mathrm{C}$ \& $\mathrm{B}_1 \mathrm{P}_1 \mathrm{~F}$ are collinear. Let each side of hexagon be equal to $x$. Let, $Q \& R$ be foot of altitudes from $P$ to base $A B \& C F$ respectively. So $R$ is centre of hexagon Now $\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{\sqrt{3} x}{2}=\sqrt{64-\frac{x^2}{4}}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{x^2}{12}=64-\frac{x^2}{4}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{4 x^2}{12}=64 \Rightarrow x=8 \sqrt{3}$ Note that circumradius of a regular hexagon = side of regular hexagon Hence $r=8 \sqrt{3} \approx 13.856$ Hence nearest integer $=14$

questions.subject: IOQM
questions.q: In parallelogram $A B C D$ the longer side is twice the shorter side. Let $X Y Z W$ be the quadrilateral formed by the internal bisectors of the angles of $A B C D$. If the area of $X Y Z W$ is 10 , find the area of $A B C D$.
questions.solution: As angle bisector and $\perp$ are same. The $\Delta$ is isosceles so all the eight triangle will be of equal area $\text { Then Required Area }=\left(\frac{2}{3} \times 10\right) \frac{\mathrm{B}}{2} \times 10=40$
